title: Dog Walking in Circles and Falling: Causes and Emergency Guide
meta_desc: Is your dog walking in circles and falling? Learn about vestibular disease, stroke signs, and why this behavior requires immediate veterinary care.
content_html:
TL;DR: Dogs walking in circles or falling over require immediate veterinary attention for potential vestibular syndrome. Provide your vet with a video of the behavior to help them diagnose causes ranging from ear infections to neurological issues quickly.

Why is my dog walking in circles, stumbling, or falling over?

When a dog begins walking in circles, stumbling, or falling over, it usually indicates a disruption in their sense of balance or neurological function known as vestibular syndrome. Potential causes include:

  • Deep ear infections
  • Inflammatory diseases
  • Strokes
  • Toxins
  • Brain tumors

How urgent is it if my dog is circling and falling?

This is considered a high-priority emergency requiring immediate veterinary intervention. You should seek emergency care especially if you notice secondary signs such as:

  • Involuntary flicking of the eyes (nystagmus)
  • A persistent head tilt
  • An inability to stand up at all

How does a video of my dog's symptoms help the veterinarian?

Capturing a brief video of your dog's movements can be an invaluable tool for your veterinarian for several reasons:

  • A video provides a clear record of the specific movements occurring at home, as dogs may behave differently in a high-stress clinic environment.
  • Visual evidence helps the medical team differentiate between various neurological conditions.
  • Seeing the symptoms firsthand can significantly speed up the diagnostic process.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Animals with chronic lesions in the cerebral cortex and diencephalon may circle compulsively, while vestibular dysfunction can cause ipsilateral falling, rolling, or circling. Animals with lesions of the midbrain, pons, and medulla oblongata may exhibit paresis or paralysis of the limbs, with deficits often more severe on the side of the lesion. Cerebellar lesions typically produce ataxia and dysmetria.

Chapter: Neurology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1217)

faqs.question: Could my dog have had a stroke?
faqs.answer: While strokes are possible in dogs, symptoms like circling and falling are more commonly caused by vestibular disease. However, only a veterinarian can distinguish between the two through a physical exam and diagnostic testing.

faqs.question: Is 'Old Dog Vestibular Disease' fatal?
faqs.answer: No, the condition itself is not usually fatal. Many dogs make a significant recovery within a few days to weeks with supportive care, though a slight head tilt may sometimes persist.

faqs.question: How can I keep my dog safe until we reach the vet?
faqs.answer: Keep your dog in a confined, padded area to prevent injuries from falling. Avoid stairs and keep them away from sharp furniture corners. Use a harness or a towel as a sling under their belly if you must help them move.
